coredata.dc:title: High-stability CsPbIBr2 nanocrystal with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot/titanium dioxide for enhancing rhodamine B photocatalytic degradation under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Bo-Chun
Liao, Wan-Hsuan
Wu, Chia-Ching
coredata.dc:description: All-inorganic CsPbX3 perovskite quantum dots have attracted substantial research interest because of their great potential for application on the photoelectronic materials. However, CsPbX3 is sensitive to polar substances and thermal treatment, both of which severely degrade the photoluminescence (PL) properties. In this study, highly stable CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals) were successfully synthesized. The nanocrystals exhibit excellent thermal stability and high chemical stability in a polar environment. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) was combined with N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals to obtain a N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite. Photocatalytic activity was assessed by testing the rate of rhodamine B (RhB) degradation under visible-light irradiation. The N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibited more efficient RhB degradation after 1 h (90% efficiency) than the N-GQDs, TiO2, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, and the N-GQD/TiO2 nanocomposite. The degradation efficiency of the N-GQDs, TiO2, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals after 1 h of approximately 2%, 5%, 3%, and 45% respectively. This enhanced photodegradation was attributed to electron and hole transport in opposite directions at the junction of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite and reduced recombination of charge carriers, which enable the rapid generation of active species (•OH radicals and •O2 − ions).

head.ce:title: High-stability CsPbIBr2 nanocrystal with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot/titanium dioxide for enhancing rhodamine B photocatalytic degradation under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: All-inorganic CsPbX3 perovskite quantum dots have attracted substantial research interest because of their great potential for application on the photoelectronic materials. However, CsPbX3 is sensitive to polar substances and thermal treatment, both of which severely degrade the photoluminescence (PL) properties. In this study, highly stable CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals) were successfully synthesized. The nanocrystals exhibit excellent thermal stability and high chemical stability in a polar environment. Titanium dioxide (TiO2) was combined with N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals to obtain a N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite. Photocatalytic activity was assessed by testing the rate of rhodamine B (RhB) degradation under visible-light irradiation. The N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibited more efficient RhB degradation after 1 h (90% efficiency) than the N-GQDs, TiO2, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, and the N-GQD/TiO2 nanocomposite. The degradation efficiency of the N-GQDs, TiO2, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals after 1 h of approximately 2%, 5%, 3%, and 45% respectively. This enhanced photodegradation was attributed to electron and hole transport in opposite directions at the junction of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite and reduced recombination of charge carriers, which enable the rapid generation of active species (•OH radicals and •O2 − ions).

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite quantum dots (PQDs) have attracted considerable attention in recent years due to their unique properties [1–4]. Perovskite semiconductors are typically organic–inorganic or solely inorganic materials with the form ABX3, where A is a cation such as organic methylammonium (MA), formamidinium (FA), or inorganic Cs+; B is a second cation such as Pb2+ or Sn2+; and X is a halide ion such as Cl−, Br−, or I−. The electronic and optoelectronic properties of these quantum dots (QDs) are interrelated and can thus be tuned by modifying the size, shape, surface, structure, and components of the QDs. Traditional QDs and PQDs have been used in applications such as solar cells [5,6], light-emitting diodes [7,8], lasers [9,10], sensors [11,12], catalysis [13,14], transistors [15], and computing [16,17]. To date, studies have mainly focused on Pb-based inorganic perovskites including CsPbI3, CsPbI2Br, CsPbIBr2, and CsPbBr3. Although these have a more stable crystal structure than CH3NH3PbX3, they still have the disadvantage of poor stability mainly due to their high sensitivity to moisture and polar solvents in ambient environments; therefore, their practical applications are limited [18,19]. For example, the low stability of CsPbX3 limits its application as a photocatalyst. Many studies have attempted to overcome this problem. For example, Zeng et al. used dual silicon nitride and silicon oxide ligands of polysilazane inorganic polymer to passivate surface defects and create a barrier layer on green-emission CsPbBr3 QDs to ensure high PL quantum yield and improve their QDs environmental stability [20]. Huang et al. protected the surface of CsPbX3 (X = Br or I) by using polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane to obtain stable solid-state perovskite-based luminophores with various emission colors [21]. Yang et al. introduced the concept of space localization for stabilizing unstable organic methylamine cations by using inorganic salts and thereby obtained a luminescent CH3NH3PbBr3/NaNO3 nanocomposite [22].

According to the World Health Organization, more than 700,000 tons of synthetic dyes are manufactured yearly worldwide, and these dyes are mainly discarded as industrial effluents [23]. In particular, rhodamine B (RhB) is extensively used as a colorant in paper, printing, textiles, and food products [24]. Due to its widespread use, RhB may be released into the environment. Animal studies of its toxicity profile have shown that RhB is carcinogenic and neurotoxic [25,26]. Moreover, biodegradation of compounds such as RhB can be slow. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have recently received increasing attention in the research and development of synthetic dye treatment technologies. AOPs such as photocatalysis, Fenton, ozonation (O3), and ultrasonic cavitation have been successfully used for wastewater treatment [27–29].

Regarding photocatalysis, Fujishima and Honda [30] first reported the photocatalytic performance of titanium dioxide (TiO2) in the photochemical oxidation and reduction of organic waste; subsequently, many other scholars have investigated the use of TiO2. TiO2 is a chemically stable, ecofriendly, large exciton diffusion length, high electron mobility, a long carrier lifetime, and inexpensive semiconductor [31,32]. TiO2 can be used in photodegradation processes [33,34]. It has a band gap of 3.2 eV; therefore, electron–hole pairs are created when TiO2 is irradiated with UV light of wavelength shorter than 385 nm [35]. These produce reactive radical species such as •OH and •O2 − [36] in solutions that reduce or oxidize the adsorbates. These active radicals and ions react with dye molecules and decompose them into carbon dioxide (CO2) and water. However, the application of TiO2 in environmental pollution remediation has been limited by its large energy band gap (~3.2 eV), which makes it unable to effectively absorb visible light. Therefore, the structure of TiO2 must be modified suitably for it to perform as an efficient photocatalyst.

In this study, the organic ligands used to synthesize CsPbIBr2, oleic acid (OA) and oleyl amine (OAm), were replaced with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) to obtain N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals. A nanocomposite comprising N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals combined with TiO2 exhibited excellent photocatalytic properties in aqueous solution.

Citric acid (CA, anhydrous), urea (99.5%), cesium iodide (CsI, 99.9%), OAm (96%), dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8%), and RhB (98%) were purchased from Acros Organics. Lead(II) bromide (PbBr, 99.998%) was purchased from Alfa. OA (90%) was purchased from Sigma. Xylene (98%) was obtained from ECHO. Finally, P25 TiO2 (80% anatase and 20% rutile) was purchased from Uniregion Bio-Tech. All chemicals were used directly without further purification.

First, 1 mmol CA and 4 mmol urea were placed in a beaker and dissolved in 5 mL of water. The CA and urea respectively served as the carbon and nitrogen source. The mixing solution was transferred to a heater plate, heated at 140 °C for 3 h, and cooled to room temperature. Alcohol was added to the beaker to rinse the solid product, and the product was then dried at 60 °C to obtain a powder. Finally, the powder was dissolved in 5 mL of DMF to obtain N-GQDs solution with a CA:urea precursor ratio of 1:4.

Perovskite CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals were prepared by dissolving 1 mmol CsI and 1 mmol PbBr2 in 2 mL of DMF solvent. This solution was then heated to 50 °C and kept at this temperature for 20 min. Subsequently, OA (0.4 mL) and OAm (0.2 mL) were added to stabilize the precursor solution, which was then heated to 90 °C and kept at this temperature for 30 min. Finally, 0.2 mL of the precursor solution was quickly added to 4 mL of xylene under vigorous stirring. Strong yellow–green emission from the CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals was immediately observed.

The process used to synthesize N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals was similar to that for CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals except that the organic ligands OA and OAm were replaced by N-GQDs. First, 1 mmol CsI and 1 mmol PbBr2 were dissolved in DMF. Then, different volumes of N-GQDs solution (1, 3, 5, and 7 mL) were added to obtain the precursor solution. Finally, 0.2 mL of the precursor solution was quickly added to 4 mL of xylene under vigorous stirring, upon which N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals were immediately created.

To evaluate how the catalytic property of TiO2 could be improved using the CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, a nanocomposite was synthesized using 4 mL of N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals and 250 mg of TiO2. Before the sintered process, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals and TiO2 was mixed at 30 min using the magnetic mixer. The mixing solution of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite was sintered at 300 °C in air and then used for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB.

The crystalline structure was investigated through two-dimensional X-ray diffraction (XRD; D8 DISCOVER with GADDS, Bruker, Germany) with CuKα radiation in the 2θ range of 20°–80°. Microstructures were analyzed using ultra-high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM; JEOL-JME-2100 F, Japan) at 100 kV. UV–visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were obtained using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (SPECORD 210 Plus, Analytik Jena, Germany). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained at room temperature using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). Infrared spectra were obtained through Fourier transform infrared spectrometry (FT-IR; NEXUS 670, Nicolet Company) with KBr as a reference. Particle size was measured using dynamic light scattering (DLS, HORIBR, Japan). The degradation efficiency was calculated as

where k is the slope corresponding to the fit line and represents the rate constant indicative of the photocatalytic efficiency [30].

Fig. 1(a) presents a photograph of the N-GQDs solution under UV light irradiation. Green emission at 538 nm was obtained for a CA:urea ratio of 1:4. The HR-TEM image shown in Fig. 1(b) indicates that the average size of the N-GQDs was 4.9 nm. The lattice distance of the N-GQDs was 0.33 nm; this value is similar to that of the graphite (002) plane [37,38].

Fig. S1 shows the PL emission spectra of N-GQDs in DMF solution under various excitation wavelengths. The spectra reveal that the N-GQDs exhibited excitation-dependent PL behavior: the PL emission peaks shifted from 536 to 541 nm as the excitation wavelength (λ ex) was changed from 400 to 460 nm. Overall, the intensity of the peaks first increased and then decreased with an increase in the excitation wavelength; the highest emission peak was at 540 nm for excitation at 440 nm.

Fig. 2 presents the XPS spectra of the N-GQDs. The C 1s spectra contain characteristic peaks, which can be fit using Gaussian fitting, at 284.2, 285.4, and 288.1 eV, which respectively correspond to the sp2 in graphene (CC) and sp3 in graphene (CN and CO) electron bonding states of the N-GQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2(a) [39,40]. The N 1s spectra of the N-GQDs, shown in Fig. 2(b), have characteristic peaks at 399.4 and 400.5 eV corresponding to the pyrrolic N and graphitic N electron bonding states of the N-GQDs, respectively [41]. From these results, N-GQDs were concluded to have been successful synthetized through a thermal treatment strategy using CA and urea as precursors.

Fig. S2(a) presents a photograph of bright-green emission from CsPbIBr2 solution under UV-light irradiation. Green emission at 540 nm was obtained under an excitation wavelength λex = 498 nm, as shown in Fig. S2(b). The lattice of CsPbX3 nanocrystals is easily penetrated by water molecules. In order to solve this problem, the organic ligands OA and OAm were replaced by N-GQDs, and N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals were successfully synthesized to solve the problem of water molecules entering the crystal lattice in the present study. The HR-TEM images presented in Fig. 3(a) show the cubic- and ellipse-like shapes of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals. The lattice spacing was calculated to be approximately 0.26 nm, corresponding to the (200) plane of N-GQD-CsPbIBr2; thus, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals were highly crystalline. Statistical analysis indicated that the average size of the prepared N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals was in the range 6.0–6.5 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(b).

Fig. S3 presents the PL emission spectra of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals synthesized at various temperatures. As the synthesis temperature was increased from 70 °C to 90 °C, the intensity of the PL emission from the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals increased; this intensity was highest at 90 °C. As the temperature was further increased to 110 °C, the intensity of the PL emission decreased. Fig. 4 shows the PL emission spectra of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals with differing N-GQDs contents. The intensity of the PL emission was highest when 1 mL of N-GQDs was used to replace OA and OAm when synthesizing the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals. This result indicates that the appropriate amount N content from N-GQDs can improve the interaction between N-GQDs and CsPbIBr2. Because N-GQDs have the same bonding mechanism as OA and OAm, they perform a similar size-tuning function in the preparation of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals.

Fig. 5 shows the FT-IR spectra of the CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, and N-GQDs. The intense C–H stretching bands near 2918 and 2851 cm−1 in the spectrum of the CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, which were obtained using the ligands of OA and OAm, indicate the existence of large amounts of OA and OAm. The peak at 1464 cm−1 corresponds to –COO stretching [42,43], as shown in Fig. 5(a). However, OA and OAm long-chain ligands are easily lost because of their weak bonding with atoms on the surface of the crystals. This leads to the CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals losing their barrier and water molecules being able to enter the lattice. To solve this problem, OA and OAm were replaced by N-GQDs to obtain N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals; the C–H stretching bands at 2918 and 2851 cm−1 were not present in the spectrum of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals [Fig. 5(b)]. Additionally, the spectrum’s absorption peak corresponding to N–H···X (X = Br− and I−) hydrogen bonds was located at 3189 cm−1 [44]. The N-GQDs, because of their many amine groups and oxygen-containing groups, can form numerous intermolecular hydrogen bonds, as reflected by the N–H absorption peak at 3220 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum [Fig. 5(c)]. The N–H absorption peak was shifted from 3220 to 3189 cm−1 in the spectrum of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, indicating that H bonds existed between –NH2 and the groups with low electronegativity [45,46]. Fig. 5(c) shows the FT-IR spectrum of the N-GQDs. The peak in this spectrum at 3431 cm−1 corresponds to O–H stretching; the same peak in the spectra of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 and CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals is at 3428 and 3427 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 1717 cm−1 results from CO stretching. The peaks corresponding to nitrogen-containing groups are at 3220 cm−1 (N–H in-plane stretching), 1616 cm−1 (-CO-NH-), and 1350 and 1441 cm−1 (C–N stretching and in-plane stretching of amide carbonyl) cm−1.

The low thermal stability of CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals limits their applications. Fig. 6 illustrates the thermal stability characteristics of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals. The PL intensity of the CsPbIBr2 and N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals heated at 100 °C decreased slightly as the heating time progressed (up to 40 min). At a heating time of 90 min, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals retained approximately 60% of their initial relative emission intensity, as determined using Eq. (4). However, the PL intensity of the CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals decreased rapidly from 40 to 90 min. These findings clearly indicated that the thermal stability of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals was considerably higher than that of the CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals.

where I 0 and I are respectively the initial and final PL intensity.

The stability of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals in ethanol solvent was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 7(a), after mixing with alcohol, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals still exhibited blue emission, whereas the CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals exhibited no luminescence under UV-light irradiation and the color of solvent mixture become orange, as shown in Fig. S4. In addition, the PL intensity of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals was almost unaffected by the mixing solvent ratio V xylene:V alcohol, as shown in Fig. 7(b). Therefore, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals were concluded to remain stable in a polar solvent.

Remarkably, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals were stable even in aqueous solution, as illustrated in Fig. 8. When water was added to xylene solution containing the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, strong blue emission from the solution’s upper layer was observed under UV-light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 8(a). After the solution was stirred, the bottom layer exhibited strong blue emission, as shown in Fig. 8(b). By contrast, the CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals dissolved in polar solution containing water within several minutes. Moreover, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals in aqueous solution exhibited a PL spectrum that was slightly red-shifted, as indicated in Fig. 9. These results confirmed the excellent aqueous stability of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals.

In this research, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals show excellent stability. Because of the N-GQDs bond to the Br- ions and I- ions by the interaction with −NH groups and the Pb2+ ions with the −COO in the precursor solution, the grains grow gradually, and the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals surface region is covered mainly with N-GQDs. The synthesis process diagram of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals as shown in Fig. S5.

CsPbIBr2 has a broad absorption range in the visible-light region, as illustrated in Fig. S6(a). However, its low stability limits its application as a photocatalyst. No study has yet focused on the use of CsPbIBr2 as a photocatalyst for dye degradation. CsPbIBr2 thin films, QDs, and nanocrystals have been used as optoelectronic devices for solar cells because of their broad absorption range [47–49]. In the present study, the excellent stability of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals in environments of various temperatures and in polar solvents is expected to enable a wide range of photocatalysis applications. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals obtained [Fig. S6(b)] includes two absorption peaks corresponding to the CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals and n-π * transition of the C–N bonds in the N-GQDs [50]. The N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals have a band gap of 2.55 eV, as shown in Fig. 10(a). Therefore, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposites were synthetized as the photocatalyst and the photocatalyst enhancing the degradation of RhB under visible light wavelengths (> 400 nm). Because only the TiO2 has low photodegradation efficiency under visible light. The N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite present the possible application prospect in aqueous environment.

The band gap structure of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals was investigated using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). Fig. 10(b) shows the valence band maximum (VBM) onset energy (E onset) and secondary electron cutoff energy (E cutoff) of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals. The energy band of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals was calculated as follows [51–53]:
(5)
E VBM = 21.22 − (E cutoff − E onset)
where for the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, E cutoff and E onset are 16.7 and 2.28 eV, respectively. According to Eq. (5), the E VBM of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals was −6.78 eV. Furthermore, combining with the band gap (Eg = 2.55 eV) of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals derived from the UV–vis spectra (Fig. S6(b) and Fig. 10(a)), the conduction band minimum (CBM) was obtained using Eq. (6), and the E CBM was -4.23 eV.
(6)
E CBM = E VBM + Eg

Fig. 11 presents TEM and HR-TEM images of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite. The TEM images provided direct evidence of the successful anchoring of N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 onto the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles. The autocorrelated HR-TEM lattice image shows a clear lattice fringe with a width of 0.341 nm; this could be assigned to the anatase (101) atomic plane of the TiO2 nanoparticles. In addition, the higher specific surface area was obtained for N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 (BET = 43.4 m2/g) compared to that of P25 TiO2 (BET = 35.4 m2/g) since a N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 on the TiO2 surface leads to a higher specific surface area.

Fig. S7 shows the XRD pattern of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite. The diffraction peaks of N-GQDs-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite are similar to the standard data of anatase-TiO2 and rutile-TiO2 nanoparticles (JCPDS no. 21-1272 and no. 21-1276) except for those of the nanocomposites at 14.83°, 21.13° and 29.82° corresponding to the (100), (110) and (200) planes of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals. Fig. 12 illustrates the photocatalytic performance of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite. The kinetics of RhB degradation in aqueous solution were studied by monitoring the decrease in intensity of the absorption peak at 554 nm in the UV–vis spectrum as the irradiation duration increased. No major shifts in the absorption peaks were observed during the degradation except for a rapid reduction in absorbance at 554 nm. This absorbance was primarily attributed to RhB remaining in the solution under our experimental conditions.

The RhB photodegradation efficacy of the N-GQDs, TiO2, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite, and the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite was investigated under visible light irradiation (wavelength > 420 nm). Fig. 13 shows plots of C/C 0 versus the irradiation duration, where C 0 and C are respectively the initial and real-time concentration of RhB. The N-GQDs alone exhibited poor performance in RhB degradation—approximately 2% degradation efficiency after visible-light irradiation for 1 h; this was attributed to the self-degradation of RhB, implying that the N-GQDs had almost no influence on the degradation process. When pure TiO2 was used as the photocatalyst, a degradation efficiency of only 5% was achieved after irradiation for 1 h owing to the large band gap (E g = 3.2 eV) of TiO2. When the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals were used, the degradation efficiency was approximately 3% after irradiation for 1 h. However, when N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite was used, approximately 47% of the RhB had degraded after 1 h of irradiation. Finally, when the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite was used, 90% degradation efficiency was achieved after 1 h of irradiation, and almost 100% degradation efficiency was achieved beyond 4 h.

To further investigate the kinetics of RhB degradation, the total organic carbon (TOC), which has been widely used to measurement the mineralization ratio of organic dyes, as shown in Fig. 14. After irradiation for 1 h, the mineralization ratio of RhB reached 74.43% with the photocatalyst of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite, which confirms that RhB could be mineralized to residual organic molecules by the N-GQDs-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite.

The photocatalytic reaction rate constant (k) is generally expressed using a pseudo-first-order model, as expressed in Eq. (3). As illustrated in Fig. 15, the degradation of RhB by the N-GQDs, TiO2, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite, and the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite under visible light irradiation followed a pseudo-first-order kinetics model. The reaction rate constant for the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite (0.81 min−1) was better than that for N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite (0.24 min−1) and much better than those for the N-GQDs (0.02 min−1), TiO2 (0.04 min−1), and the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals (0.03 min−1). The reaction rate constant for the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite was 3.33 times higher than that for N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite and almost 20 times higher than that for TiO2, as shown in Fig. 15.

High recyclability is advantageous in a catalyst because it aids its commercialization and industrialization. To explore the reusability of our catalytic system, the efficiency of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite in the degradation of RhB was monitored over six cycles. The catalytic performance of the recovered catalyst was discovered to be nearly the same in the first, second, and third cycles, as illustrated in Fig. 16. Thus, no major changes were discovered in the photocatalytic activity of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite treated with simple washing and baking between the consecutive reusability cycles. The photocatalytic activity of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite was slightly lower for the fourth to sixth cycles- 78%, 71% and 65% degradation efficiency, respectively, under visible light irradiation. This result was caused by mass loss of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite; its mass was 50.0, 49.2, 48.4, 46.2, 44.6, and 42.4 mg after the first to sixth cycles. In addition, Fig. S8 shows the XRD patterns of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite before and after six cycles for RhB degradation. The diffraction peaks of N-GQDs-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite after six cycles for RhB degradation (as shown in Fig. S8(b)) are almost the same with the as-synthesized N-GQDs-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite (as shown in Figs. S7 and S8(a)), and no secondary or unknown phases were detected. The N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite is thus an efficient and economical photocatalyst for practical applications.

The effect of dye pH on degradation by a photocatalyst must be understood for practical applications. Fig. 17 shows the effect of pH on RhB degradation using the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite. At a pH of 3.0, the RhB degradation efficiency was approximately 95% after irradiation for 1 h and almost 100% beyond 2 h. Notably, approximately 90% degradation efficiency was achieved under irradiation for 1 h when the pH was 5. The RhB degradation efficiency under irradiation for 1 h was only slightly affected when the pH was changed from 3 to 5.

Electrostatic attraction occurred between the positive charge of cationic RhB and the negatively charged surface of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite at an alkali pH. However, at pH = 9 and 11, the degradation efficiency was 22% and 29% under irradiation for 1 h, respectively, as illustrated in Fig. 17. Coulombic repulsion was reported for cationic dyes (such as RhB and methylene blue) at pH > 9, and this repulsion can reduce the number of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and thereby decrease the photodegradation efficiency. Therefore, coulombic repulsion between the surface of the negatively charged N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite and hydroxide ions could result in a lower concentration of •OH in the solution and thus lower efficiency of photodegradation [54]. Using the pseudo-first-order model, the photocatalytic reaction rate constant k was calculated (Fig. 17). The degradation efficiency clearly decreased with an increase in the pH. In an acidic system (pH = 3), k was approximately 2.5 times that in the neutral system (pH = 7.0).

In order to investigate the role of the different active species (•O2 −, •OH, and h+) in the catalytic system which was generated under visible light irradiation, the trapping experiment was proceed. The purpose of trapping experiment is to investigate the roles of active species obviously. The trapping experiments of active species were conducted using different scavengers during the photocatalytic degradation processes of RhB with the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite, and the results are presented in Fig. 18.

Three scavengers were used in this study: (1) ethylenediaminetetracetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na; 0.02 g/L) was used to capture h+; (2) isopropanol (0.02 g/L) was added to capture •OH; and (3) benzoquinone (0.01 g/L) was employed to capture •O2 − [55]. The different scavengers clearly resulted in different effects on RhB, as illustrated in Fig. 18. The RhB degradation efficiency of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite was 30%, 50%, and 60% in the presence of EDTA-2Na, isopropanol, and benzoquinone, respectively, under irradiation for 1 h. It is indicated that the BQ, IPA and EDTA-2Na revealing that all the •O2 −, •OH and h+ acted as the main active species in photocatalytic reaction for degrading RhB. However, under irradiation for 2–5 h, the degradation efficiency of photocatalytic was still increased when in the presence of BQ or IPA, revealing that •O2 − and •OH are not main predominant species in photocatalytic reaction for degrading RhB. Meanwhile, no notable decreasing of degradation efficiency was observed when using EDTA-2Na as scavenger, which indicates that h+ was a main active species and played a important role in the photocatalytic reaction.

The obtained results indicated a possible mechanism underlying the photocatalytic activity of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite; this mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 1. TiO2 cannot be excited by visible light because its band gap is 3.2 eV. During the photocatalytic degradation of RhB, excitation of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite under visible-light irradiation results in the production of electron–hole pairs. The electrons photoexcited from the conduction band (CB) of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals rapidly move to the CB of TiO2 and then combine with adsorbed O2 to generate superoxide radical anions (•O2 −), which drive oxidization and reduction reactions. In addition, the •O2 − can be converted into H2O2, and this H2O2 produces the strong oxidant •OH. Photoexcited holes from the valence band (VB) of TiO2 may transfer to that of a N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystal, where the photogenerated holes can better combine with adsorbed H2O/OH− ions to form •OH, which directly oxidizes RhB. Simultaneously, the RhB traps visible light to excite electrons from the highest-occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to the lowest-unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) from which they are transferred to the CB of a N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals. Additionally, •O2 − is produced by these electron transfers. Therefore, the photogenerated electron–hole pairs are separated at the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 junction. Finally, the •O2 − and •OH radicals complete the degradation of RhB into oxidized products such as CO2, H2O, and inorganic ions. This mechanism is the cause of the fast electron transitions and the excellent photocatalytic performance of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals.

Fig. 19(a) illustrates the degradation of RhB by N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite synthesized at various temperatures. For synthesis temperatures of 250 °C and 300 °C, the RhB degradation rate was 60–90% at 1 h, respectively; at higher synthesis temperatures, the degradation rate was lower than 90%. Therefore, the optimal temperature for synthesizing the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite is 300 °C. Fig. 19(b) shows the degradation of RhB when using various amounts of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite. The degradation of RhB was 90% when 50 mg of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite was added.

The effect of four initial RhB concentrations (1, 5, 9, and 13 mg/L) was investigated when using 50 mg of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite to determine the optimal working concentration of photocatalyst [Fig. 19(c)]. Increasing the RhB concentration from 1 to 13 mg/L resulted in a major decrease in the degradation efficiency. RhB was almost completely removed within 3 and 4 h when the RhB concentration was 1 and 5 mg/L, respectively. At low concentrations, RhB molecules adsorbed on the photocatalyst’s surface underwent a surface reaction with •O2 − or •OH on the surface. However, at RhB concentrations of 9 and 13 mg/L, the degradation rate was lower, and RhB could not be fully removed even for a degradation duration of more than 5 h. At high RhB concentrations, shielding of light by the RhB solution led to poor interaction of photons with the catalyst, resulting in insufficient generation of electron–hole pairs and, in turn, insufficient •O2 − or •OH. In addition, at higher concentrations, crowding of molecules on the catalyst surface may have blocked the active sites and thus negatively affected the photocatalytic degradation rate. Table 1 presents a comparison of various TiO2-based photocatalysts investigated in several studies and used for the degradation of RhB. Clearly, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalyst synthesized in the present study exhibited the highest degradation rate.

In this study, N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals were successfully prepared with N-GQDs used as the ligand. Because of their high chemical stability in environments of various temperatures and in polar solvents, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals are expected to be useful in photocatalysis applications. The N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibits excellent photocatalytic properties in aqueous solution, with an RhB degradation rate 20 times that of TiO2 under visible-light irradiation. The reaction rate constant of the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2/TiO2 nanocomposite (0.81 min−1) is higher than those of TiO2, the N-GQDs, the N-GQD-CsPbIBr2 nanocrystals, and a N-GQD/TiO2 nanocomposite. This is because a sufficient number of hydroxyl radical and superoxide radical anions are generated to effectively degrade RhB.
